publisher.publisher-name: The Korean Society of Veterinary Science

title-group.article-title: The biomechanical and biological effect of supercooling on cortical bone allograft

article-meta.aff: 1Department of Small Animal Clinical Sciences, University of Florida College of Veterinary Medicine, Gainesville, FL 32611, United States of America.
2Department of Veterinary Surgery, College of Veterinary Medicine, Konkuk University, Seoul 05029, Korea.
3KU Center for Animal Blood Medical Science, Konkuk University, Seoul 05029, Korea.

author-notes.corresp: Corresponding author: Hun-Young Yoon. Department of Veterinary Surgery, College of Veterinary Medicine, Konkuk University, 120 Neungdong-ro, Gwangjin-gu, Seoul 05029, Korea. yoonh@konkuk.ac.kr

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2023 The Korean Society of Veterinary Science
permissions.copyright-holder: The Korean Society of Veterinary Science

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial License (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0) which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: The need for a storage method capable of preserving the intrinsic properties of bones without using toxic substances has always been raised. Supercooling is a relatively recently introduced preservation method that meets this need. Supercooling refers to the phenomenon of liquid in which the temperature drops below its freezing point without solidifying or crystallizing.

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: The purpose of this study was to identify the preservation efficiency and applicability of the supercooling technique as a cortical bone allograft storage modality.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: The biomechanical effects of various storage methods, including deep freezing, cryopreservation, lyophilization, glycerol preservation, and supercooling, were evaluated with the three-point banding test, axial compression test, and electron microscopy. Additionally, cortical bone allografts were applied to the radial bone defect in New Zealand White rabbits to determine the biological effects. The degree of bone union was assessed with postoperative clinical signs, radiography, micro-computed tomography, and biomechanical analysis.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: The biomechanical properties of cortical bone grafts preserved using glycerol and supercooling method were found to be comparable to those of normal bone while also significantly stronger than deep-frozen, cryopreserved, and lyophilized bone grafts. Preclinical research performed in rabbit radial defect models revealed that supercooled and glycerol-preserved bone allografts exhibited significantly better bone union than other groups.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Considering the biomechanical and biological superiority, the supercooling technique could be one of the optimal preservation methods for cortical bone allografts. This study will form the basis for a novel application of supercooling as a bone material preservation technique.

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Critical-size bone defects have been reported to be caused by various conditions, including disuse osteopenia, non- or delayed union, bone tumors, severe trauma, and infection [123]. Surgical reconstruction of large bone defects is significantly challenging not only because of the anatomical complexity but also due to the lack of bone grafting materials with satisfying biomechanical and biological properties [4]. Especially in critical-size defects of long bones subjected to multidirectional cyclic loads, bone materials should be able to withstand mechanical stresses and resist failure at sites where structural support is required [5].

Bone allografts have been used by orthopedic surgeons for centuries to provide structural stability and support for bone regeneration [126]. Even though autologous bone grafts are considered the gold standard in bone grafting, the usage of autologous tissue is restricted by anatomical limitations, varying quality, donor site morbidity, and pain [1]. Allogeneic bone grafts have become viable alternatives by avoiding the limitations of bone autografts [7]. Allogeneic bone grafts come in a variety of forms, including cortical, cancellous, and demineralized bone matrices. Cortical bone allografts possess excellent structural stability and can resist higher loads in comparison to cancellous bone allografts, whose coarse structure has little mechanical support [8]. These mechanical properties enable the usage of cortical bone allografts in spinal fusion, dental implants, high tibial osteotomy, and limb reconstruction [1].

Various storage techniques for cortical allografts have been developed, including preservation by freezing, lyophilization, honey, and glycerol [8910]. Traditional preservation techniques, such as freezing and lyophilization, have been reported to result in the successful incorporation of bone allografts; however, these techniques have the potential to compromise structural stability as a result of the freeze-thaw or lyophilization process [11121314]. The glycerol preservation technique has been proposed to address these limitations [81015]. Glycerol is water soluble and has a low molecular weight, allowing it to replace water at the molecular level and fit into the available spaces within bone tissue [16]. These properties keep the bone grafts fully hydrated and allow them to be stored at room temperature for periods exceeding five years [8]. Biomechanical studies demonstrated that glycerol-preserved bone grafts maintain the native properties of bone, in contrast to freeze-dried bone grafts, which are more brittle with a lower modulus of rupture [14]. Glycerol has been effectively utilized as a preservative in bone matrices [1718]. However, glycerol is reported to exhibit low cytotoxicity in almost all organisms [19202122]. Therefore, additional time is required to thoroughly remove the glycerol before surgical implantation, and there is a possibility that the glycerol may remain on the bone graft material, leading to delayed bone healing.

In recent years, there has been continuous interest and effort in applying supercooling technology for biomaterial preservation [232425]. Numerous reports have been published demonstrating the successful application of the supercooling method for preserving cells, tissues, and organs [242627]. Supercooling is the process of lowering the temperature of a biomaterial below its freezing point without forming damaging ice crystals, which inevitably affects the microstructures of biomaterials by water expansion upon freezing [28]. Due to its physical properties, supercooling preservation has advantages over traditional storage techniques in that the tissue can be stored for an extended period in a fully hydrated and unfrozen state, which prevents the tissue from being damaged by storage processes [25].

The biomechanical properties and clinical applications of the aforementioned cortical bone allograft preservation techniques have been widely investigated [9172930]. However, only a few studies have evaluated relative differences in the biomechanical and biological properties of various preservation methods [8910]. In particular, no previous study has applied supercooling technology to bone tissue preservation and evaluated the impact of supercooling on the bone union of cortical bone allografts. Therefore, further comparative studies on the effects of various cortical bone storage methods on cortical bone are needed to expand the storage options and promote the clinical application of cortical bone allografts. This study aimed to comprehensively compare the biomechanical and biological effects of various preservation techniques on cortical bone allografts.

sec.title: Collection of cortical bone samples
sec.p: Cortical bone samples were procured from the humeral, radial, and tibial bones of 36 New Zealand White (NZW) rabbits, weighing approximately 3.5 kg, used for other non-musculoskeletal experiments at the time of sacrifice. The proximal and distal ends (1 cm) of the radius and humeral bones were removed using an oscillating saw to facilitate bone marrow removal, and the remaining midparts were collected. One-centimeter-long tibial bone samples were procured by cutting the tibial bone 1.5 cm to 2.5 cm away from the distal end of the tibia. All decellularization processes were performed as previously described [47]. Sixty humeral and radial bone samples were collected for the three-point bending tests, and 60 tibial bone samples were obtained for the axial compression tests. Twelve tibial bone samples were additionally collected for electron microscopy analysis. This study was approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee at Konkuk University (approval number: KU21125).

sec.title: Experimental groups and preservation methods
sec.p: The ten humeral and radial bone samples and twelve tibial bone samples were randomly assigned into six groups, respectively, in each group: control, deep freezing (DF), cryopreservation (CR), lyophilization (LY), glycerol preservation (GP), and supercooling preservation (SU). The DF and CR groups were preserved in a deep freezer (CLN-51U; Nihon Freezer, Japan) at −80°C and liquid nitrogen at −196°C, respectively. The LY group was subjected to primary drying for 12 h at −40°C under vacuum (4 mTorr). During secondary drying, the shelf temperature was increased to 22°C at a rate of 0.2 °C/min and kept under vacuum for 12 h. Lyophilization procedures were performed using a freeze-dryer (LP03; Ilsin Bio Base, Korea). The GP group was preserved in glycerol solution (mixture of glycerol and 99% ethanol [Daejung Chemicals & Metals Co., Ltd., Korea] in a ratio of 4:1) at room temperature. The samples were submerged in glycerol solution at a 1:10 bone mass to solution volume ratio, sealed by parafilm, and stored in a shaded place. The SU group was preserved in a supercooled state using a supercooling refrigerator (−5°C, Wondercool; Supercooler Co, Ltd, Korea). The samples were stored in a petri dish and sealed with parafilm to prevent moisture exposure and contamination. The supercooling refrigerator was not opened or closed during the storage period to minimize the impact that made the supercooling state unstable. All samples except the control group samples were preserved for 60 days. The control group was used for tests without a storage period after decellularization.

sec.title: 1. Three-point bending and axial compression tests
sec.p: The mechanical properties of the humeral and radial bones were evaluated by three-point bending tests using a universal testing system (Instron 3369; Instron Corp, USA). A load was applied to the center of each specimen at a rate of 0.5 mm/sec until fracture occurred (Fig. 1). The two supporting points were positioned at the points where the bone sample and jig structure met. The span length, the distance between the two supporting points, was 40 mm. The loading force (stress) and displacement (strain) were measured using Instron Bluehill software. The following parameters were determined by analyzing the recorded stress-strain curves: 1) Stiffness (N/mm): the slope of the linear portion of the elastic curve, and 2) Maximum load (N): the highest load reached before failure occurs.

caption.title: The photo images of biomechanical tests using a universal testing system. The three-point bending test is performed to evaluate the biomechanical properties of the humerus (A) and radius (B). The supporting points (orange circles) are placed at the points where the bone sample meets the concrete support holder. The loading point (blue circle) is positioned at the midpoint of the bone sample. The two supporting points are 40 mm apart (black double arrowhead). The tibial bone is applied with axial compressive force (red arrow) to measure the maximum load before failure (C).

sec.p: For the axial compression tests, tibial specimens were placed on standard platens with the cut side facing up and down and loaded to failure at a 0.3 mm/sec rate. The compressive force was measured until fracture occurred to estimate the maximum load (N) the tibial bone sample could withstand.

sec.title: 2. Electron microscopy assessment
sec.p: Structural changes on the cortical surfaces of tibial bone samples were also analyzed using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, SU8010; Hitachi High-Tech, Japan). Two tibial bone samples, selected from each group, were dried under vacuum at 20°C for 24 h and then coated with silver using an ion sputter (MC1000; Hitachi High-Tech). The cranial center of the bone surface was viewed at ×100 and ×300 magnifications, and the microstructure was captured.

sec.title: In vivo rabbit radial defect assessment
Cortical bone allograft samples
sec.p: Cortical bone samples were procured from the right radial bone of 32 NZW, weighing approximately 3.5 kg, used for other non-musculoskeletal experiments at the time of sacrifice. One-centimeter-long radial bone samples were procured by cutting the radial bone 1.5 cm to 2.5 cm away from the distal end of the right radius using an oscillating saw. After decellularization, bone allograft was sterilized by 70% (v/v) ethanol for 10 min, followed by 1% (v/v) antibiotics/antimycotics solution (penicillin 100 IU/mL, streptomycin 100 mg/mL, amphotericin B 0.25 ug/mL) for an hour.

sec.title: Experimental groups and preservation methods
sec.p: The cortical bone allografts were randomly assigned into four groups, with eight samples in each group: CR, LY, GP, and SU. The preservation of each method was the same as in the previous in vitro study. All cortical bone grafts were preserved for 60 days.

sec.title: Surgical procedure
sec.p: Rabbits were pre-medicated with cefazoline (30 mg/kg, subcutaneous [SC]), enrofloxacin (5 mg/kg, SC), tramadol (5 mg/kg, SC), meloxicam (1 mg/kg, SC), and butorphanol (0.2 mg/kg, SC). After induction with xylazine (5 mg/kg, intramuscular [IM]) and ketamine (35 mg/kg, IM), a supraglottic airway device (V-gel Rabbit R-4; Docsinnovent Ltd., UK) was inserted for respiratory ventilation. Anesthesia was maintained with isoflurane in oxygen. Under sterile conditions, a 10 mm long segmental radial bone defect was made at the distal portion of the diaphysis by cutting with an oscillating saw. Autologous cancellous bone grafts were harvested from the anterior region of the iliac crest and used to fill the medullary cavity of the cortical bone allograft. The prepared cortical bone allograft was tightly fit into the bone defect and fixed with an eight-hole, 1.2-mm locking plate system, in which two self-tapping screws were placed at the implanted allografts and six self-tapping screws, three proximally and three distally located from the allograft, were placed at the recipient radial bone (Fig. 2). Postoperatively, cefazoline (30 mg/kg, SC, once per day [SID]), enrofloxacin (5 mg/kg, SC, SID), tramadol (5 mg/kg, SC, SID), and meloxicam (1 mg/kg, SC, SID) were administered and the surgical site was disinfected daily with 10% povidone-iodine solution for 2 weeks.

caption.title: Intraoperative photograph (A, B) and radiographic images (C, D) of cortical bone allograft implantation. A segmental bone defect is reconstructed using a cortical bone allograft and plate-screw system. The 4-week postoperative radiographs show that two screws are placed at the implanted cortical allograft, and six screws, three proximal and three distal from the allograft, are placed at the recipient radial bone. The scale bars indicate 5 mm.

sec.title: Analysis of clinical signs
sec.p: Postoperative clinical signs, mainly related to pain, were evaluated 1, 2, and 3 weeks after surgery. The rabbits were allowed to walk freely in the room while observational clinical examinations were performed. The clinical outcomes were examined for the standing position, gait with movement, swelling of the operated limb, pain on palpation of the operated limb, behavior, body temperature, and appearance of the incision. Each criterion for clinical outcomes was scored based on the cumulative numerical scoring system devised by Stasiak et al. [31]. The sum of scores from a minimum of 0 to a maximum of 19 points was rated. The lower the score, the fewer clinical signs, which could be interpreted as being close to normal.

sec.title: Radiologic assessment
sec.p: Radiographic evaluations of the operated right front limbs were performed at 4 and 8 weeks after cortical bone graft implantation surgery. All radiographs were obtained in the craniocaudal, lateral, and lateral-oblique directions (Fig. 3). The radiographs were examined for bone union at the proximal and distal graft-host junction, callus formation, callus remodeling, and implant fixation. Each criterion for radiologic evaluation was scored (Table 1). This scoring system was devised based on previous studies [93233]. The criterion for radiographic callus formation was established as bone bridging around the fracture line. Callus remodeling was defined as bone healing with the shape and radiodensity of the callus formed around the graft-host interface returning to that of the original bone. The sum of scores from a minimum of 0 to a maximum of 15 points was rated. A higher score indicated better cortical bone allograft incorporation.

caption.title: Radiographic images of the supercooling preserved cortical bone allograft 4 weeks (A, B, C) and 8 weeks (D, E, F) post-operatively. Anterior-posterior (A, D), medio-lateral (B, E), and oblique (C, F) views. The radiographs are evaluated for bone healing and fixating implants. Red, green, white, and yellow arrows show mild bridge (< 50%) with minimal callus, moderate bridge (> 50%) with mild callus, full bone bridge with visible fracture line, and absence of fracture, respectively. The implanted plate-screw system is intact without any failure.
Overview of the radiographic scoring system

tr.td: No new bone in the fracture line - nonunion
Full bone bridge with visible fracture line
No new bone in the fracture line - nonunion
Full bone bridge with visible fracture line

sec.p: In addition to the scoring system, the percentage of fracture lines with active callus formation out of all graft-host interfaces was measured in the postoperative 4-week radiographic examinations. In the postoperative 8-week radiographic examination, sixteen interfaces in each group were evaluated to calculate the ratio of unioned graft-host interfaces. Radiographic union was determined to be present when there was complete continuity in the graft-host interface.

sec.title: Micro-computed tomographic (micro-CT) assessment
sec.p: Micro-CT was performed to obtain more detailed information on the bone union (Fig. 4). Following 8 weeks of postoperative radiography, mice were euthanized by CO2 asphyxiation radial and ulnar bones were removed together from all 32 front limbs. Bone samples were scanned with a high-resolution micro-CT instrument (SkyScan 1273; Bruker, Belgium). Sixteen interfaces in each group were evaluated to calculate the nonunion rate. Computed tomographic nonunion was considered present when there was a discontinuity in the graft-host interface with severe bone resorption activity. The computed tomographic criteria selected for the bone union analysis are described in Table 2. These criteria were selected based on the work of Stacy et al. [34] and Gómez-Barrena et al. [35]. Based on these parameters (callus formation, cortical allograft quality, and bone allograft incorporation), the bone union scale was generated with a score range of 0 to 11 points.

caption.title: Micro-computed tomographic images of the cryopreserved (A), lyophilized (B), glycerol preserved (C), and supercooling preserved (D) bone allografts in a rabbit 8 weeks post-operatively. Red arrows show marked cortical resorption and deformation of cryopreserved allograft. Lyophilized allograft reveals nonunion with fracture (green arrow). Distal graft-host junction of glycerol preserved allograft shows continuous callus formation with visible fracture line (white arrow). Supercooling preserved allograft shows new bone formation with indistinct fracture line (yellow arrow).
Overview of the micro-computed tomographic scoring system

tr.td: Callus continuous but fracture still apparent
Callus with the same density as cortical bone (no fracture line)
Callus continuous but fracture still apparent
Callus with the same density as cortical bone (no fracture line)
Marked resorption of cortical, deformation, or fracture
Cortical resorption present but not marked, no deformation or fracture present

sec.title: Biomechanical analysis
sec.p: The radial bone was separated from the ulna to measure the biomechanical properties of bone union between the cortical bone allograft and host bone. Three-point bending tests were performed using a universal testing system to determine the load-carrying capacity. After installing the samples on support holders, the load was applied to the center of the implanted cortical bone allograft at a rate of 0.3 mm/sec until fracture occurred. The loading force (stress) and displacement (strain) were measured.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Three-point bending and axial compression tests were conducted to analyze the biomechanical properties of the cortical bone allograft and bone union. The micro-CT data were analyzed to identify differences in the degree of bone union. The Kruskal–Wallis test was performed, followed by the Mann–Whitney post hoc test for multiple comparisons. The clinical signs scoring data and the radiologic evaluation were analyzed to check the clinical effect and degree of the bone union of the cortical allografts in the four groups using repeated-measures analysis of variance (ANOVA) with post hoc Tukey’s test. A p value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. Statistical analysis was performed using IBM SPSS (v25.0; SPSS Inc., USA). The experimental values are presented as the means ± SDs.

sec.title: Biomechanical analysis
sec.p: Table 3 shows the values for the biomechanical test of cortical bone samples. In the three-point bending tests of the humerus, the LY group demonstrated a significantly lower stiffness than the GP and SU groups, and the SU group showed a significantly higher maximum load than the DF, CR, and LY groups. The control groups showed a significantly higher stiffness and maximum load than the DF, CR, and LY groups.

caption.title: The results of the three-point bending test on the humerus and radius and axial compression test on the tibia

table-wrap-foot.p: A p value lower than 0.05 indicates that there are significant differences between the groups at a 95% confidence level. Data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation.
ST, stiffness; ML, maximum load; DF, deep freezing; CR, cryopreservation; LY, lyophilization; GP, glycerol preservation; SU, supercooling preservation.
aSignificant difference compared to the control group (p < 0.05); bSignificant difference compared to the GP group (p < 0.05); cSignificant difference compared to the SU group (p < 0.05).

sec.p: The radial bone three-point bending tests showed that the GP group had a significantly higher stiffness than the CR group and a greater maximum load than the DF, CR, and LY groups. The SU group had significantly higher stiffness and maximum load than the CR and LY groups. The control groups showed a significantly higher stiffness than the CR and LY groups and a greater maximum load than the DF, CR, and LY groups. No significant differences were found between the DF, CR, and LY groups and between the control, GP, and SU groups.

In the axial compression tests of the tibia, the GP group showed a significantly higher maximum load than the LY group, and the SU group revealed a significantly greater maximum load than the DF, CR, and LY groups. The control groups showed a significantly higher maximum load than the DF, CR, and LY groups. No significant differences were found between the DF, CR, and LY groups and between the control, GP, and SU groups.

sec.title: Electron microscopic assessment
sec.p: Interpretation of the results was performed by comparing the cortical surface of each group to that of the control group (Fig. 5). The control group samples, which were not processed for preservation, had smooth regular bone surfaces without distinct pits, erosions, and microcracks. The DF group samples had numerous small surface microcracks with moderate irregularity. Microcracks and macrocracks were developed on the roughened surfaces of the CR group samples. The LY group samples demonstrated moderate erosion and irregularity with numerous microcracks. The GP and SU group samples showed slight surface irregularities with no distinct evidence of microcracks.

caption.title: Electron microscopy images of the normal, deep-frozen, cryopreserved cortical bone, and lyophilized, glycerol-preserved, and supercooling-preserved cortical bone samples. Field emission scanning electron microscopy is performed on silver-coated samples. The control group reveals no evidence of microcracks, erosion, or pits. Micro- (white arrows) and macro-cracks (yellow arrow) are observed in the deep-frozen and cryopreserved cortical allografts. An extensively deformed cortical surface is observed in the LY group. The GP and SU groups maintain a cortical surface morphology similar to that of the control group. The red dotted box in the image of GP (×100) shows the inner area of the white dotted box in control (×100). The scale bars on the 100× and 300× magnification images indicate 150 µm and 50 µm, respectively.
caption.p: DF, deep freezing; CR, cryopreservation LY, lyophilization; GP, glycerol preservation; SU, supercooling preservation.

sec.title: In vivo rabbit radial defect assessment
sec.p: Table 4 shows the values of in vivo biological tests, including clinical sign assessment, radiography, micro-CT, and biomechanical analysis.

caption.title: The results of the in vivo rabbit radial defect assessment.

table-wrap-foot.p: A p value lower than 0.05 indicates that there are significant differences between the groups at a 95% confidence level. Data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation.
ST, stiffness; ML, maximum load; micro-CT, micro-computed tomographic; DF, deep freezing; CR, cryopreservation; LY, lyophilization; GP, glycerol preservation; SU, supercooling preservation.
aSignificant difference compared to the CR group (p < 0.05); bSignificant difference compared to the GP group (p < 0.05); cSignificant difference compared to the SU group (p < 0.05).

sec.title: Clinical scores
sec.p: All rabbits recovered their normal gait within 2 weeks. One rabbit in LY group revealed a small incision site dehiscence at 10 days postoperatively. The dehiscence was healed uneventfully after medical treatment. At 3 weeks postoperatively, the CR, GP, and SU groups had no clinical signs, with zero scores. Four rabbits in the LY group showed mild pain on palpation of an operated limb at 3 weeks. The clinical scores of the LY group were significantly higher than those of the other groups. No significant differences were found between the CR, GP, and SU groups. Postoperative complications, such as plate-screw system failure, fever, and surgical site infection, were not observed in any of the 32 rabbits.

sec.title: Radiographic scores
sec.p: The LY group showed significantly lower radiographic scores than all other groups. In addition, there were significant differences between the SU and CR groups. The rates of active callus formation at 4 weeks postoperatively were 25% (4/16), 18.75% (3/16), 87.5% (14/16), and 87.5% (14/16) in the CP, LY, GP, and SU groups, respectively. The rates of the radiographic union at 8 weeks postoperatively were 43.75% (7/16), 18.75% (3/16), 68.75% (11/16), and 68.75% (11/16) in the CP, LY, GP, and SU groups, respectively.

sec.title: Micro-CT scores
sec.p: The GP and SU groups showed significantly higher values than the LY group. No significant differences were found between the GP and SU groups. The 3D volume rendering images revealed that the nonunion rates were 31.25% (5/16) for the CR group, 62.5% (10/16) for the LY group, 12.5% (2/16) for the GP group, and 18.75% (3/16) for the SU group.

sec.title: Biomechanical analysis
sec.p: The three-point bending test measured the structural strength of bone union between bone allograft and host bone. Two radial bones implanted with lyophilized allografts exhibited immediate fracture separation upon detachment of the plate-screw system and ulnar bone from the radius, indicating complete nonunion. The LY group revealed a significantly lower maximum load than the SU group. Stiffness results showed no significant differences between all groups.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: Cortical bone allografts have the advantage of being able to resist high loads and be readily used without compromising donor site morbidity for autografts. In addition, cortical allografts can be prepared with various sizes and morphologies depending on where the cortical bone was taken. For the successful reconstruction of large defects of weight-bearing bones, cortical bone allografts should be strong and rigid enough to support the weight. Failure to withstand the loading forces could cause graft or fixating implant fracture or micromotion in the graft-host interface, leading to poor revascularization, fibrous tissue formation, continuous pain, and nonunion [3637].

The bone union of implanted bone grafts occurs through osteoconductive, osteoinductive, and osteogenic mechanisms, which can be influenced by cortical allograft preparation and storage methods [35]. In general, the preparation process for cortical allografts removes most of the growth factors and viable cells that foster osteoinduction and osteogenesis. Thus, cortical allografts are considered osteoconductive bone grafts with limited osteoinductive potential [3]. The micromotion caused by the mechanical instability of cortical allografts could interfere with neovascularization into the allografts, indicating that changes in mechanical properties caused by the preservation process have a close relationship with the osteoconductivity of cortical allografts [38].

Various preservation methods have been introduced to preserve the intrinsic properties of cortical bone, including deep freezing, cryopreservation, preservative solution, and lyophilization [891030]. However, conventional methods have certain limitations, mainly due to the properties of water molecules. As cortical bone is frozen, water in the bone structure crystallizes into an open hexagonal form and expands, resulting in microcracks on the bone microstructure [39]. Additionally, the surface of cortical bone cools faster than the inner area, leading to a temperature gradient across the bone. This gradient induces ‘thermal stress’ and changes the biomechanical properties of bone [29]. Andrade et al. demonstrated that freezing caused the denaturation of the collagen component of cortical bone, and this qualitative alteration was aggravated proportional to decreasing temperature and freezing duration [11]. Lyophilization and subsequent rehydration have been reported to cause a significant reduction in biomechanical strength and structural changes [839]. Voggenreiter et al. [40] demonstrated that lyophilization caused microcracks running parallel to the collagen fibers, which was suggested to be the reason for the reduced biomechanical properties of bone grafts. The deformation mechanism of freeze-dried cortical bone was demonstrated to be plastic deformation, not brittle fracture [3941].

In the current study, the relatively common glycerol preservation and newly introduced supercooling techniques were compared to the conventional methods. The findings of this in vitro investigation unveiled that the storage methods involving freezing and lyophilization storage methods, such as deep freezing, cryopreservation, and lyophilization groups, exhibit comparatively reduced biomechanical values and greater structural deformation than glycerol and supercooling preservation methods. Considering the characteristics of water molecules, the observed deteriorations in the intrinsic properties of the conventional method groups may be associated with the expansion of water and temperature gradients within the cortical bone. Especially in lyophilized cortical bone, the moderately deformed cortical structure might be further affected by sublimation and dehydration processes. These findings corroborate the earlier research conducted by Ozcelikkale et al. [42], highlighting that the destabilization of the collagen matrix is primarily due to ice formation, with no significant impact observed when subjected to low temperature in the absence of ice. Glycerol and supercooling preservation methods have advantages over conventional techniques in that freezing and lyophilization processes were not included in the preparation process.

In line with in vitro findings, most of the supercooling-preserved cortical allografts were successfully incorporated into the host bone. These allografts showed extensive callus formation at 4 weeks, followed by moderate bone union at 8 weeks. The glycerol-preserved cortical allografts also induced a significant amount of bone union. The strength of bone union between the cortical allografts and host bone was directly measured by three-point bending tests. The glycerol- and supercooling-preserved cortical allografts achieved the highest values, and the lyophilized allografts showed the lowest values. Among the four dogs of the LY group that showed mild pain on palpation of an operated limb at 3 weeks, cortical allografts of two dogs showed complete nonunion, and the other two revealed marked resorption, deformation, and fracture of implanted bone grafts. This suggests that the biomechanical impairments observed in lyophilized cortical bone allografts could be related to the instability of the allograft-host bone structure, potentially contributing to pain at the surgical site, which in turn may lead to delayed union or nonunion.

These experiments validate glycerol and supercooling preservation methods as superior options for storing cortical bone allografts. Additionally, the authors noted the close correlation between the biomechanical and biological attributes of cortical bone allografts and recognized that the natural occurrence of ice crystal formation may be a pivotal factor to consider in bone graft preservation. However, both techniques have distinct drawbacks. For glycerol-based preservation, the preservative solution, a mixture of glycerol and ethanol, should be completely removed before surgical implantation due to the potential toxicity and osmotic stress [43]. There is a possibility that bone union may be reduced by the remnant preservatives. For the supercooling preservation technique, special equipment is required to keep the cortical bone samples in a supercooled state. In particular, the ice nucleation phenomenon occurs more easily in solid materials than in pure water [44]. Therefore, it is not easy to maintain the supercooled state of cortical bone allografts. In addition, because the storage temperature of the supercooling preservation method is relatively higher than that of freezing preservation, bone grafts might be vulnerable to storage degradation and infection.

This study has a limitation in that only one storage period (60 days) was analyzed. Therefore, the impact of storage duration on the cortical bone allografts was not taken into account for evaluating the effects of each preservation method. According to the work of Andrade et al. [11], the structural properties of preserved cortical bone grafts could be changed in a time-dependent manner. Furthermore, histological analysis was not performed in this study, a crucial component for the precise assessment of microstructural and compositional changes, including the formation of micro-fracture formation and collagen denaturation. Further studies are warranted to elucidate the influence of supercooling preservation on the structural and compositional characteristics of cortical bone allografts and to establish their correlations with biomechanical changes.

In conclusion, these in vitro and in vivo preclinical studies demonstrated that freezing and lyophilization processes impaired the biomechanical properties and caused microstructural deformation. On the other hand, the supercooling and glycerol preservation technique best preserved cortical bone allograft biomechanically and biologically. Considering that the storage process is simple and cytotoxic preservatives could be avoided, supercooling storage could be one of the optimal options among several existing preservation methods. This study will form the basis for a novel application of supercooling as a bone material preservation technique. However, due to the storage temperature being higher than the conventional freezing storage, cortical bone allografts preserved by supercooling may have a relatively short shelf life. Further research is required to improve the supercooling technique and its clinical utilization.

fn.p: Conflict of Interest: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

list-item.p: Data curation: Kim M, Yoon HY.
Writing - review & editing: Kim M, Yoon HY.
